title: Carrot cake
subtitle: Oil-based spiced cake with cream cheese frosting
excerpt: Carrot cake uses oil rather than butter, which gives it a moist, dense crumb that improves with a day's rest. Finely grated carrot provides sweetness and moisture; mixed spice and cinnamon provide the flavour backbone. The cream cheese frosting is applied thickly.
sourceNotes: Carrot cake in its modern form (oil-based, spiced, with cream cheese frosting) became popular in British domestic cookery in the 1970s, following its earlier American mainstream popularisation. Recipes appear across women's magazines and household cookbooks from that decade onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the sponge layers uniced. Defrost at room temperature and frost before serving. Cream cheese frosting does not freeze well.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake improves significantly with a day's rest — bake and frost the day before serving. Keep refrigerated once frosted.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake made with neutral-tasting oil rather than butter. Oil stays liquid at room temperature, which keeps the crumb moist for longer than a butter cake. Oil-based cakes do not need creaming — the batter comes together by simply whisking wet ingredients into dry, which also makes them more straightforward to prepare.

content.text: , which means the batter comes together by whisking rather than creaming. Grate the carrot finely so it disappears into the crumb rather than sitting in visible shreds. The frosting should be cold when it goes on — warm cream cheese frosting slides off.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Grease two 23 cm round tins and line the bases. Whisk {{eggs}}, {{light-brown-sugar}}, and {{sunflower-oil}} together in a large bowl for 1 minute. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{baking-powder}}. Stir through the {{carrot}} and {{walnuts}} if using. Divide between the tins and bake for 30–35 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the tops spring back when pressed. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes before turning out.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{icing-sugar}} together first until pale, then beat in the {{cream-cheese}} and {{vanilla-extract}} — starting with butter keeps the frosting from becoming too loose. Do not over-beat once the cream cheese is in. Spread a third on the base layer, sandwich with the second sponge, and spread the remainder on top.
